summary: Joe Reis argues that the tension between orthodox data modeling approaches and the pressure to 'move fast' creates technical debt that teams rarely pay down. He advocates for understanding conventional frameworks like relational and dimensional modeling before breaking rules, warning that ignorance of orthodoxy leads to either misapplication or costly reinvention.
key_points: Orthodox data modeling approaches (relational, dimensional, Data Vault) exist because decades of refinement have proven they work for 99% of companies
The Big Data era decoupled intentional modeling from practice, leading to 'set it and forget it' data messes
Understanding orthodoxy lets you know which rules to follow and which to break deliberately
Resume-driven development leads teams to adopt Big Tech solutions that don't fit their scale
The future data professional must be both 'old school' AND 'new school'
money_quotes: There is never enough time to do it right, but there is always enough time to do it over.
Stubborn table-centric people are the data equivalent of Flat Earthers.
Don't think you're smart enough to ignore orthodoxy without understanding the consequences.
Dogmatism is almost more dangerous than premature optimization.
For many teams these days, thinking is often replaced by managing a Jira backlog and slogging through 2-week sprints, all in the name of 'shipping.'
key_insight: True speed in data work comes from understanding orthodox approaches deeply enough to know when following or breaking them serves your specific situation.
